Question.content: Which of the following best summarizes the cell theory?
options: All organisms are made of atoms
All living things are made of cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and cells arise from pre-existing cells
Cells can arise spontaneously from non-living matter
Only animals are made of cells

Explanation.content:
The Three Pillars of Cell Theory

The answer is B. The cell theory has three core statements developed over time by Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow.

The 3 statements:
1. All living organisms are composed of one or more cells
2. The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of life
3. All cells arise from pre-existing cells (Omnis cellula e cellula — Virchow, 1858)

Spontaneous generation was disproven by Pasteur — cells do NOT arise from non-living matter.

Answer.content: All living things are made of cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and cells arise from pre-existing cells

Question.content: The scientist who first observed cells using a microscope and coined the term 'cell' was:

Explanation.content:
Robert Hooke — Father of the Cell

The answer is Robert Hooke (1665). He observed thin slices of cork under a compound microscope and named the tiny compartments "cells" because they reminded him of monk's rooms (cellulae).

Key scientists timeline:
• Hooke (1665) — coined "cell"
• Leeuwenhoek (~1674) — first to observe living cells (bacteria, protozoa)
• Schleiden & Schwann (1838–39) — all organisms made of cells
• Virchow (1858) — cells from pre-existing cells

Question.content: Which of the following statements is INCONSISTENT with modern cell theory?
options: All cells arise from pre-existing cells
Cells can arise spontaneously from non-living matter
The cell is the basic unit of life
All organisms are made of cells

Explanation.content:
What Cell Theory REJECTS

The answer is B. The idea that life can arise spontaneously from non-living matter is called abiogenesis or spontaneous generation. Louis Pasteur definitively disproved this in 1859 with his swan-neck flask experiment.

⚠️ Exam trap: Spontaneous generation is the classic "trick" option. Modern cell theory explicitly states cells ONLY come from pre-existing cells — never from non-living material.

Answer.content: Cells can arise spontaneously from non-living matter

Question.content: Which scientist contributed the idea that ALL plants are made of cells?

Explanation.content:
Schleiden vs Schwann

The answer is Matthias Schleiden (1838). He concluded that all plant tissues are composed of cells. A year later, Theodor Schwann extended this to animals.

Easy way to remember:
• Schleiden → Shrubs (plants) 🌿
• Schwann → Swans are animals 🦢
• Together they said: all organisms are made of cells.

Question.content: Which of the following is the MOST fundamental difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
options: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus; prokaryotes do not

Explanation.content:
The Defining Difference

The answer is B. The word "eukaryote" literally means "true nucleus" (eu = true, karyon = nucleus). The presence of a membrane-bound nucleus is the defining feature.

Both cell types HAVE:
• Cell membrane, ribosomes, DNA, cytoplasm

Only Eukaryotes HAVE:
• Membrane-bound nucleus, mitochondria, ER, Golgi apparatus

Note: Both have ribosomes, but different sizes — prokaryotes: 70S; eukaryotes: 80S

Answer.content: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus; prokaryotes do not

Question.content: Which of the following organisms is prokaryotic?

Explanation.content:
Prokaryote Examples

The answer is Escherichia coli (E. coli). It is a bacterium — the most classic prokaryote.

Classification:
• E. coli — bacterium → Prokaryote ✅
• Amoeba — protozoan → Eukaryote
• Yeast — fungus → Eukaryote
• Mushroom — fungus → Eukaryote

All bacteria and archaea are prokaryotes.

Question.content: The ribosome size in prokaryotic cells is:

Explanation.content:
Ribosome Sizes

The answer is 70S. Prokaryotic ribosomes are smaller (70S) than eukaryotic ribosomes (80S). The "S" stands for Svedberg units — a measure of sedimentation rate.

Ribosome subunits:
• Prokaryote 70S = 50S + 30S subunits
• Eukaryote 80S = 60S + 40S subunits

This difference is medically important — antibiotics like streptomycin target the 70S ribosome, killing bacteria without harming human (80S) ribosomes.

Question.content: Which of the following structures is found in prokaryotes but NOT in eukaryotes?

Explanation.content:
Plasmids — Prokaryote Exclusive

The answer is Plasmid. Plasmids are small, circular, extrachromosomal DNA molecules found in bacteria. They are separate from the main chromosome and can replicate independently.

Why plasmids matter:
• Carry genes for antibiotic resistance
• Used extensively in genetic engineering
• Can be transferred between bacteria (conjugation)

Eukaryotes do NOT naturally have plasmids (though they are used in laboratory genetic work).

Question.content: The nucleolus, found inside the nucleus, is primarily responsible for:
options: Synthesis of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and assembly of ribosome subunits

Explanation.content:
The Nucleolus

The answer is B. The nucleolus is a dense region inside the nucleus where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) genes are actively transcribed and ribosome subunits begin assembly.

Path of a ribosome:
1. rRNA made in nucleolus
2. Combined with ribosomal proteins → subunits
3. Exported to cytoplasm via nuclear pores
4. Subunits assemble → functional ribosome for translation

Answer.content: Synthesis of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and assembly of ribosome subunits

Question.content: The rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) differs from the smooth ER (SER) in that it:

Explanation.content:
RER vs SER

The answer is B. The RER appears rough because ribosomes are studded on its outer surface. These ribosomes synthesize proteins that are threaded into the RER lumen for processing and export.

Comparison:
• RER — has ribosomes; makes secretory/membrane proteins
• SER — no ribosomes; lipid synthesis, drug detox (liver), Ca²⁺ storage (muscle cells)

Question.content: Mitochondria are described as semi-autonomous organelles because:
options: They can move inside the cell
They have their own DNA and ribosomes

Explanation.content:
Semi-Autonomous Organelles

The answer is B. Mitochondria (and chloroplasts) contain their own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes — evidence for the endosymbiotic theory, which proposes they were once free-living bacteria engulfed by ancestral eukaryotic cells.

Endosymbiotic Theory (Lynn Margulis):
Mitochondria resemble alpha-proteobacteria in DNA, ribosomes, and double membrane structure. They are "semi-autonomous" because they still depend on the nucleus for many proteins.

Answer.content: They have their own DNA and ribosomes

Question.content: The process by which a lysosome digests its own cell's damaged organelles is called:

Explanation.content:
Autophagy — Cellular Housekeeping

The answer is Autophagy. Auto = self, phagy = eating. In autophagy, damaged organelles or proteins are enclosed in a membrane (autophagosome), which fuses with a lysosome for digestion.

Don't confuse:
• Autophagy — digests own organelles/proteins ✅
• Phagocytosis — engulfs external particles (bacteria)
• Apoptosis — programmed cell death (whole cell)
• Autolysis — lysosome ruptures, destroys whole cell

Question.content: Which organelle is responsible for the synthesis of lipids and steroid hormones?

Explanation.content:
Smooth ER — The Lipid Factory

The answer is Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER). The SER houses enzymes for lipid biosynthesis, including phospholipids and cholesterol, and is abundant in steroid-secreting cells (adrenal cortex, gonads).

SER functions summary:
1. Lipid and steroid hormone synthesis
2. Drug and toxin detoxification (liver cells)
3. Calcium ion storage and release (muscle cells)
4. Carbohydrate metabolism

Question.content: Which of the following correctly describes the flow of a newly synthesized secretory protein through the cell?
options: Ribosome → RER → Golgi apparatus → Vesicle → Plasma membrane

Explanation.content:
The Secretory Pathway

The answer is B. This is the classic secretory pathway — sometimes called the "default pathway" for membrane and secreted proteins.

Step-by-step:
1. Ribosome on RER — translates the protein into the RER lumen
2. RER — initial folding and N-linked glycosylation
3. Vesicle — transport to Golgi apparatus
4. Golgi apparatus — further modification, sorting, packaging
5. Secretory vesicle — carries protein to plasma membrane (exocytosis)

Answer.content: Ribosome → RER → Golgi apparatus → Vesicle → Plasma membrane

Question.content: Which of the following structures is found in plant cells but NOT in animal cells?

Explanation.content:
Plant Cell Exclusive Structures

The answer is Cell wall and chloroplasts. These are the two most commonly tested plant-only structures.

Plant vs Animal comparison:
StructurePlantAnimal
Cell wall (cellulose)✅❌
Chloroplasts✅❌
Large central vacuole✅❌
Centrioles❌✅

Question.content: The plant cell wall is primarily composed of:

Explanation.content:
Cell Wall Composition

The answer is Cellulose. Plant cell walls are made of long cellulose microfibrils embedded in a matrix, providing structural support and rigidity.

Cell wall composition by organism:
• Plants — Cellulose
• Fungi — Chitin
• Bacteria — Peptidoglycan
• Animals — No cell wall

Question.content: Centrioles play a critical role in:
options: Forming the mitotic spindle during cell division

Explanation.content:
Centrioles & Cell Division

The answer is Forming the mitotic spindle during cell division. Centrioles are cylindrical structures made of microtubules, found in the centrosome of animal cells. They organize the spindle fibers that pull chromosomes apart during mitosis and meiosis.

Note: Most plant cells can still divide without centrioles, using alternative spindle-organizing mechanisms. This is why centrioles are absent in most plant cells.

Answer.content: Forming the mitotic spindle during cell division

heading: Section 5: Biological Membranes & The Fluid Mosaic Model

Question.content: The fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane was proposed by:

Explanation.content:
History of Membrane Models

The answer is Singer and Nicolson (1972). Their fluid mosaic model replaced the older Davson-Danielli sandwich model, incorporating evidence from electron microscopy and freeze-fracture studies.

Previous model:
• Davson & Danielli (1935) — proposed the sandwich model: protein-lipid-protein layers. This was disproved because it placed all proteins on the surface, which did not match experimental evidence.

Singer & Nicolson showed proteins are embedded throughout the bilayer.

Question.content: Integral (intrinsic) membrane proteins differ from peripheral (extrinsic) proteins in that they:
options: Are only found on the outer leaflet
Are loosely attached to the membrane surface

Explanation.content:
Integral vs Peripheral Proteins

The answer is Span through the lipid bilayer. Integral proteins (also called transmembrane proteins) are embedded within the hydrophobic core of the bilayer and require detergents to be extracted.

Comparison:
• Integral proteins — penetrate or span the bilayer; function as channels, receptors, carriers
• Peripheral proteins — loosely associated with the surface; can be removed by mild treatments (high salt, pH change); often function in signaling and cytoskeletal attachment

Question.content: The glycocalyx is formed by carbohydrates attached to membrane lipids and proteins. Its primary function is:

Explanation.content:
The Glycocalyx

The answer is Cell recognition, adhesion, and signaling. The glycocalyx is a carbohydrate-rich coat on the extracellular surface, formed by glycoproteins and glycolipids.

Functions of glycocalyx:
1. Cell-cell recognition — immune cells identify foreign cells
2. Cell adhesion — cells stick to each other and to the extracellular matrix
3. Receptor activity — binds signaling molecules (hormones, growth factors)
4. Protection — cushions the cell surface

Question.content: Membrane fluidity INCREASES when:
options: Proportion of saturated fatty acids increases
Proportion of unsaturated fatty acids increases
More cholesterol is added at high temperatures

Explanation.content:
What Controls Membrane Fluidity?

The answer is C. Unsaturated fatty acids have kinked tails (from double bonds) that prevent tight packing, keeping the membrane more fluid.

Fluidity factors:
• ↑ Temperature → ↑ Fluidity
• ↑ Unsaturated fatty acids → ↑ Fluidity (kinks prevent packing)
• ↑ Saturated fatty acids → ↓ Fluidity (straight chains pack tightly)
• Cholesterol = fluidity buffer — increases fluidity at low temps, decreases it at high temps

Answer.content: Proportion of unsaturated fatty acids increases

Question.content: The phospholipid bilayer is described as selectively permeable. This means:
options: No molecules can pass at all
Small nonpolar molecules pass freely; large and charged molecules do not

Explanation.content:
Selective Permeability

The answer is C. The hydrophobic core of the bilayer allows only certain molecules through.

Permeability rules:
• Freely pass — small nonpolar molecules (O₂, CO₂, N₂), small uncharged polar (water, ethanol)
• Pass slowly — large uncharged polar molecules (glucose)
• Cannot pass unaided — ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻), large charged molecules, proteins

Ions and large polar molecules require protein channels or carriers.

Answer.content: Small nonpolar molecules pass freely; large and charged molecules do not

Question.content: The two leaflets (inner and outer) of the plasma membrane differ in lipid composition. This asymmetry is maintained by an enzyme called:

Explanation.content:
Membrane Asymmetry

The answer is Flippase. Flippases (and floppases) are ATP-dependent enzymes that move specific phospholipids from one leaflet to the other, maintaining membrane asymmetry.

Outer leaflet: Phosphatidylcholine, sphingomyelin
Inner leaflet: Phosphatidylserine, phosphatidylethanolamine

During apoptosis, phosphatidylserine flips to the outer leaflet — this is a "eat me" signal for macrophages.

Question.content: A uniport transport protein moves:
options: Two substances in the same direction
A single substance in one direction

Explanation.content:
Uniport, Symport, Antiport

The answer is A single substance in one direction. Uniporters transport only one type of molecule across the membrane.

Carrier protein types:
• Uniport — 1 substance, 1 direction (e.g., GLUT glucose transporter)
• Symport — 2 substances, same direction (e.g., Na⁺-glucose cotransporter)
• Antiport — 2 substances, opposite directions (e.g., Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger)

Answer.content: A single substance in one direction

Question.content: In the intestine, glucose absorption across the intestinal epithelium uses a Na⁺-glucose cotransporter. This is an example of:

Explanation.content:
Symport in Action

The answer is Symport. The Na⁺-glucose cotransporter (SGLT) moves both Na⁺ and glucose in the same direction (into the cell). The energy comes from the Na⁺ gradient (maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺ pump) — this is called secondary active transport.

Important concept:
Glucose moves against its concentration gradient (low → high), but the energy comes from Na⁺ moving down its gradient — so no ATP is directly used. This is secondary active transport via symport.

Question.content: Facilitated diffusion differs from simple diffusion in that it:
options: Requires membrane transport proteins but no energy

Explanation.content:
Facilitated Diffusion

The answer is C. Facilitated diffusion is still passive (no ATP), but it requires protein channels or carrier proteins to help large or charged molecules cross the membrane down their gradient.

Comparison table:
FeatureSimpleFacilitated
Protein needed?NoYes
ATP needed?NoNo
MoleculesSmall nonpolarGlucose, ions

Answer.content: Requires membrane transport proteins but no energy

Question.content: Endocytosis that specifically engulfs large solid particles (such as bacteria) is called:

Explanation.content:
Types of Endocytosis

The answer is Phagocytosis (cell eating). It is used by immune cells (macrophages, neutrophils) to engulf bacteria and cellular debris.

Endocytosis types:
• Phagocytosis — engulfs large solid particles ("cell eating")
• Pinocytosis — engulfs extracellular fluid and small solutes ("cell drinking")
• Receptor-mediated endocytosis — specific molecules bind receptors on cell surface → clathrin-coated pit forms → internalized (e.g., LDL uptake)

Question.content: The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump is classified as primary active transport because:
options: It uses the gradient of another ion
It directly uses ATP hydrolysis to drive transport

Explanation.content:
Primary vs Secondary Active Transport

The answer is B. Primary active transport directly couples ATP hydrolysis to the movement of ions. The Na⁺/K⁺ pump is a classic example.

Why the pump is important:
1. Maintains low Na⁺ inside / high K⁺ inside
2. Creates membrane potential (electrical gradient) for nerve/muscle function
3. Drives secondary active transport (symporters use the Na⁺ gradient created)
4. Regulates cell volume

Answer.content: It directly uses ATP hydrolysis to drive transport

Question.content: When a cell releases substances by fusing intracellular vesicles with the plasma membrane, the process is called:

Explanation.content:
Exocytosis

The answer is Exocytosis. In exocytosis, vesicles containing proteins or other materials fuse with the plasma membrane and release their contents to the extracellular space.

Examples of exocytosis:
• Secretion of insulin by pancreatic beta cells
• Release of neurotransmitters from nerve terminals
• Secretion of digestive enzymes by the pancreas
• Export of antibodies by plasma cells

Question.content: A red blood cell placed in a hypotonic solution (lower solute concentration than the cell) will:

Explanation.content:
Osmosis and Cell Behavior

The answer is Swell and may burst. In a hypotonic solution, water moves into the cell by osmosis (from low solute → high solute), causing it to swell. Red blood cells can lyse (burst) — this is called hemolysis.

Osmosis cheat sheet:
• Hypotonic solution → water enters cell → swells/bursts (lysis)
• Hypertonic solution → water leaves cell → shrinks (crenation in RBCs)
• Isotonic solution → no net water movement → normal shape

Question.content: Aquaporins are membrane channel proteins that specifically facilitate the transport of:

Explanation.content:
Aquaporins — Water Channels

The answer is Water molecules. Aquaporins are specialized channel proteins that allow rapid, selective passage of water across the membrane — much faster than simple osmosis through the lipid bilayer.

Importance of aquaporins:
• Kidney tubules — water reabsorption
• Red blood cells — rapid water flux
• Plant roots — water uptake
• Discovered by Peter Agre (Nobel Prize 2003)

Question.content: The general structure of a phospholipid consists of:
options: Glycerol + two fatty acids + phosphate group + polar head group

Explanation.content:
Phospholipid Structure

The answer is B. Glycerophospholipids (the most common type) have a glycerol backbone with two fatty acid chains esterified at C1 and C2, and a phosphate group at C3 linked to a polar head group.

Structure diagram (simplified):
Polar head group
      |
   Phosphate  ← hydrophilic head
      |
   Glycerol
    /     \
FA tail   FA tail  ← hydrophobic tails

Answer.content: Glycerol + two fatty acids + phosphate group + polar head group

Question.content: Oleic acid (18:1, n-9) is classified as a:

Explanation.content:
Reading Fatty Acid Notation

The answer is Monounsaturated fatty acid. The notation 18:1 means 18 carbons with 1 double bond. One double bond = monounsaturated.

Fatty acid notation: C:D (n-x)
• C = number of carbons
• D = number of double bonds
• n-x = position of first double bond from methyl end

Examples:
• 18:0 = Stearic acid (saturated)
• 18:1(n-9) = Oleic acid (monounsaturated, olive oil)
• 18:2(n-6) = Linoleic acid (polyunsaturated, essential)

Question.content: The two parent essential fatty acids that humans cannot synthesize are:

Explanation.content:
The Two Parent Essential Fatty Acids

The answer is Linoleic acid (omega-6) and alpha-linolenic acid (omega-3). Humans lack the desaturase enzymes needed to introduce double bonds beyond C9, making these acids dietary essentials.

From these, the body can make:
• Linoleic (18:2, n-6) → Arachidonic acid (20:4, n-6) → prostaglandins, leukotrienes
• Alpha-linolenic (18:3, n-3) → EPA → DHA (important in brain and retina)

Deficiency causes: poor growth, skin problems, impaired brain development.

Question.content: Cholesterol is the precursor for the synthesis of all of the following EXCEPT:

Explanation.content:
Cholesterol as a Precursor

The answer is Glycogen. Glycogen is a polysaccharide (carbohydrate) — it is NOT derived from cholesterol. Cholesterol is a sterol lipid, and it gives rise to all steroid-based molecules.

Cholesterol is the precursor for:
1. Bile acids — aid fat digestion in the intestine
2. Steroid hormones — cortisol, aldosterone, estrogen, testosterone, progesterone
3. Vitamin D — synthesized in skin upon UV light exposure
4. Membrane cholesterol — regulates fluidity

Question.content: Chylomicrons are lipoprotein particles that are synthesized in:

Explanation.content:
Chylomicrons — Dietary Fat Transporters

The answer is Intestinal epithelial cells. Chylomicrons are the largest lipoproteins, formed in intestinal cells after fat absorption. They transport dietary triglycerides and cholesterol via the lymph (lacteals) to the bloodstream.

Lipoprotein origin summary:
• Chylomicrons — intestinal cells (dietary fats)
• VLDL — liver (endogenous triglycerides)
• LDL — derived from VLDL in bloodstream
• HDL — liver and intestine

Question.content: Sphingolipids differ from glycerophospholipids in that they have a backbone of:

Explanation.content:
Sphingolipids

The answer is Sphingosine. Unlike glycerophospholipids (glycerol backbone), sphingolipids are built on sphingosine — a long-chain amino alcohol derived from serine and palmitoyl-CoA.

Types of sphingolipids:
• Sphingomyelin — found in myelin sheath of nerves; has phosphocholine head
• Cerebrosides — have a single sugar (glucose or galactose); found in brain
• Gangliosides — complex oligosaccharide head; important in cell signaling

Question.content: Atherosclerosis is primarily associated with the excessive buildup of which lipoprotein in arterial walls?

Explanation.content:
Atherosclerosis & LDL

The answer is LDL. Elevated LDL cholesterol is the primary driver of atherosclerosis — LDL particles enter the artery wall, become oxidized, and are engulfed by macrophages, forming foam cells that build up as plaques.

⚠️ Atherosclerosis progression:
1. LDL enters damaged arterial wall
2. LDL is oxidized → macrophages engulf it → foam cells
3. Foam cells accumulate → fatty streak → plaque
4. Plaque hardens → narrows artery → heart attack or stroke

Question.content: The second law of thermodynamics states that in any spontaneous process:
options: The total entropy of the universe decreases
The total entropy of the universe increases
Energy can be fully converted without loss

Explanation.content:
Second Law of Thermodynamics

The answer is C. The second law states that entropy (disorder) of the universe always increases in any spontaneous process — energy conversions are never 100% efficient; some energy is always lost as heat.

Two laws side by side:
• 1st Law — Energy cannot be created or destroyed (conservation of energy)
• 2nd Law — Entropy of the universe always increases (disorder increases)

Example: When ATP is hydrolyzed, not all the energy goes to useful work — some is lost as heat, increasing overall entropy.

Answer.content: The total entropy of the universe increases

Question.content: Given: ΔH = −50 kJ/mol, ΔS = +0.10 kJ/mol·K, T = 300 K. What is the value of ΔG?

Explanation.content:
Calculating ΔG: Gibbs Equation

The answer is −80 kJ/mol. Apply the Gibbs free energy equation:

Formula:
\( \Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S \)

Step-by-step:
\( \Delta G = -50 \text{ kJ/mol} - (300 \text{ K} \times 0.10 \text{ kJ/mol·K}) \)
\( \Delta G = -50 - 30 \)
\( \Delta G = -80 \text{ kJ/mol} \)

Since ΔG is negative → the reaction is spontaneous (exergonic).

Question.content: The type of bond in ATP whose hydrolysis releases a large amount of free energy is called a:

Explanation.content:
The High-Energy Bond in ATP

The answer is Phosphoanhydride bond. ATP has three phosphate groups. The bond between the β and γ phosphates (and between α and β phosphates) is a phosphoanhydride bond — these release approximately 30.5 kJ/mol when hydrolyzed.

Why is this bond so energetic?
• Products (ADP + Pᵢ) are more stable than ATP
• Resonance stabilization after hydrolysis
• Electrostatic repulsion between negative phosphates is relieved

Question.content: Creatine phosphate (phosphocreatine) functions as an energy reserve by:
options: Rapidly regenerating ATP from ADP in muscle during intense exercise
Producing NADH for the electron transport chain

Explanation.content:
Creatine Phosphate — Instant Energy Reserve

The answer is B. When muscle ATP is rapidly depleted during intense exercise, creatine phosphate donates its phosphate group to ADP, instantly regenerating ATP — a reaction catalyzed by creatine kinase.

\( \text{Creatine-P} + \text{ADP} \xrightarrow{\text{creatine kinase}} \text{Creatine} + \text{ATP} \)

This buffer lasts only a few seconds but buys time until glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation can catch up.
This is why creatine supplements are popular in sports nutrition.

Answer.content: Rapidly regenerating ATP from ADP in muscle during intense exercise

Question.content: A reaction is said to be endergonic when:

Explanation.content:
Endergonic vs Exergonic

The answer is ΔG is positive. An endergonic reaction requires an input of free energy — it is non-spontaneous under standard conditions.

Summary:
• ΔG < 0 → Exergonic (releases energy, spontaneous)
• ΔG > 0 → Endergonic (requires energy, non-spontaneous)
• ΔG = 0 → Equilibrium (no net reaction)

In cells, endergonic reactions are driven by coupling them to exergonic reactions like ATP hydrolysis.

Question.content: In biochemistry, oxidation is defined as:

Explanation.content:
Oxidation & Reduction in Biochemistry

The answer is Loss of electrons or hydrogen. Biological oxidations typically involve removal of hydrogen atoms (H⁺ + e⁻) from substrates, captured by carriers like NAD⁺ and FAD.

Mnemonic — OIL RIG:
Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons)
Reduction Is Gain (of electrons)

\( \text{NAD}^+ + 2H \rightarrow \text{NADH} + H^+ \) ← NAD⁺ is reduced (gains H)

Question.content: The standard free energy of hydrolysis of ATP (ΔG°') is approximately:

Explanation.content:
ATP Hydrolysis Energy Value

The answer is −7.3 kcal/mol (−30.5 kJ/mol). This is a key value to memorize. ATP sits in the "middle" of the phosphoryl transfer potential scale — it can donate phosphate to lower-energy molecules and receive phosphate from higher-energy ones.

High-energy phosphate compounds (ΔG°' of hydrolysis):
PEP: −14.8 kcal/mol (highest) → ATP: −7.3 → Glucose-6-P: −3.3 kcal/mol (lowest)

This ordering allows ATP to act as the universal energy intermediary.

Question.content: What is the structural difference between a nucleoside and a nucleotide?
options: A nucleoside has a phosphate group; a nucleotide does not
A nucleotide has a phosphate group; a nucleoside does not
A nucleoside has a double-stranded structure
A nucleotide contains only the sugar and base

Explanation.content:
Nucleoside vs Nucleotide

The answer is B. A nucleoside = base + sugar. A nucleotide = base + sugar + one or more phosphate groups.

Examples:
• Adenosine = adenine + ribose → nucleoside
• AMP = adenine + ribose + 1 phosphate → nucleotide
• ADP = adenine + ribose + 2 phosphates → nucleotide
• ATP = adenine + ribose + 3 phosphates → nucleotide

Memory trick: NucleotIDE = nucleosIDE + phosphate

Answer.content: A nucleotide has a phosphate group; a nucleoside does not

Question.content: Deoxyadenosine is a nucleoside composed of:

Explanation.content:
Deoxyribonucleoside Naming

The answer is Adenine + deoxyribose. The prefix "deoxy" tells you the sugar is deoxyribose (missing the 2'-OH). No phosphate = nucleoSIDE not nucleoTIDE.

Naming guide:
• Base alone: adenine, guanine, cytosine...
• + ribose → nucleoside: adenosine, guanosine...
• + deoxyribose → deoxynucleoside: deoxyadenosine, deoxythymidine...
• + phosphate(s) → nucleotide: AMP, ADP, ATP...

Question.content: Which of the following is a difference between DNA and RNA?
options: DNA contains uracil; RNA contains thymine
DNA is single-stranded; RNA is double-stranded
DNA contains deoxyribose and thymine; RNA contains ribose and uracil
DNA and RNA have identical base compositions

Explanation.content:
DNA vs RNA — Key Differences

The answer is C. This option correctly states both differences: the sugar type AND the unique base for each.

FeatureDNARNA
SugarDeoxyriboseRibose
Unique baseThymine (T)Uracil (U)
StrandsDoubleSingle
LocationNucleusNucleus + Cytoplasm
StabilityMore stableLess stable

Answer.content: DNA contains deoxyribose and thymine; RNA contains ribose and uracil

Question.content: NAD⁺ (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) is important in metabolism as it functions as:
options: A coenzyme and electron carrier in oxidation-reduction reactions
A structural component of cell membranes

Explanation.content:
NAD⁺ — Electron Carrier

The answer is B. NAD⁺ is a coenzyme derived from niacin (vitamin B3). It accepts electrons and hydrogen (as hydride, H⁻) during oxidation of metabolic substrates, becoming NADH, which then donates electrons to the electron transport chain.

\( \text{NAD}^+ + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{NADH} + H^+ \)

Other important electron carriers:
• FAD/FADH₂ — from riboflavin (B2)
• NADP⁺/NADPH — used in biosynthetic reactions (e.g., fatty acid synthesis)

Answer.content: A coenzyme and electron carrier in oxidation-reduction reactions

Question.content: Cyclic AMP (cAMP) is derived from ATP and functions as:
options: An intracellular second messenger in hormone signaling

Explanation.content:
cAMP — Second Messenger

The answer is B. cAMP (cyclic adenosine monophosphate) is produced from ATP by adenylyl cyclase (activated by G-proteins). It amplifies hormone signals inside the cell by activating Protein Kinase A (PKA).

The cAMP signaling cascade:
Hormone (epinephrine) → binds receptor → G-protein activated → Adenylyl cyclase activated → ATP → cAMP → PKA activated → cellular response (e.g., glycogen breakdown)

Answer.content: An intracellular second messenger in hormone signaling

Question.content: Watson and Crick's model of DNA (1953) proposed that the two DNA strands are:

Explanation.content:
Watson-Crick DNA Model

The answer is Antiparallel and complementary. One strand runs 5'→3' while the other runs 3'→5' (antiparallel). The bases pair specifically: A with T, G with C (complementary).

Key features of the Watson-Crick double helix:
1. Right-handed double helix (B-form)
2. Two antiparallel, complementary strands
3. ~10 base pairs per complete turn
4. Major and minor grooves alternate
5. Bases are on the inside, sugar-phosphate backbone on the outside
6. Based on X-ray crystallography by Rosalind Franklin

Question.content: If one strand of DNA has the sequence 5'-ATCGTA-3', the complementary strand will be:

Explanation.content:
Writing the Complementary Strand

The answer is 3'-TAGCAT-5'. The complementary strand must be antiparallel (3'→5') and have matching bases (A↔T, G↔C).

Step-by-step:
Original:      5'- A  T  C  G  T  A -3'
Complement:       |  |  |  |  |  |
New strand:    3'- T  A  G  C  A  T -5'
The complement reads 3'-TAGCAT-5' (same as 5'-TACGAT-3' if reversed).

Question.content: According to Chargaff's rules, in a double-stranded DNA molecule with 30% adenine, the percentage of guanine would be:

Explanation.content:
Chargaff's Rules

The answer is 20%. Chargaff's rules state: %A = %T and %G = %C. All four bases must add up to 100%.

Calculation:
A = 30% → T = 30% (since A = T)
A + T = 60%
Remaining for G + C = 100% − 60% = 40%
G = C = 20% each

So the answer is G = 20%

Question.content: The three forms of DNA double helix include A-DNA, B-DNA, and Z-DNA. The form found under normal physiological conditions in cells is:

Explanation.content:
Forms of DNA

The answer is B-DNA. B-DNA is the right-handed helix described by Watson and Crick, which is the predominant form under physiological (aqueous) conditions.

Three forms comparison:
• B-DNA — right-handed, ~10 bp/turn, physiological ✅
• A-DNA — right-handed, shorter/wider, found in dehydrated conditions
• Z-DNA — left-handed, zigzag backbone, found in regions with alternating GC repeats; may play a role in gene regulation

Question.content: In the DNA double helix, the base pairs are stabilized primarily by:

Explanation.content:
Stabilizing Forces in DNA

The answer is Hydrogen bonds between complementary bases. A–T pairs share 2 hydrogen bonds; G–C pairs share 3 hydrogen bonds.

Two major stabilizing forces:
1. Hydrogen bonds between base pairs (A=T and G≡C)
2. Base stacking interactions — hydrophobic and van der Waals forces between stacked aromatic rings

Don't confuse: Phosphodiester bonds link nucleotides within a strand (backbone). Hydrogen bonds hold the TWO strands together.

Question.content: Which type of RNA makes up the structural and catalytic core of ribosomes?

Explanation.content:
rRNA — Ribosomal RNA

The answer is rRNA. Ribosomal RNA is the most abundant RNA in the cell (~80%). It makes up the structural scaffold of ribosomes and has catalytic activity — the peptidyl transferase activity that forms peptide bonds is actually carried out by rRNA, making ribosomes a ribozyme.

RNA type summary:
• mRNA — carries genetic code from DNA to ribosome
• tRNA — brings amino acids; reads mRNA codons
• rRNA — structural and catalytic component of ribosomes ✅
• snRNA — involved in mRNA splicing in the spliceosome

Question.content: The anticodon of tRNA is found in which structural region?

Explanation.content:
tRNA Structure

The answer is Anticodon loop. The anticodon is a 3-nucleotide sequence located in the anticodon loop of tRNA. It base-pairs with the complementary codon on mRNA during translation.

Key tRNA features:
• 3' CCA tail (acceptor stem) — where the amino acid attaches
• Anticodon loop — reads mRNA codon ✅
• D-arm — interacts with aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase
• T-arm — helps tRNA bind to the ribosome

tRNA is ~75 nucleotides long and folds into a cloverleaf secondary structure.

Question.content: Which of the following describes heterogeneous nuclear RNA (hnRNA)?
options: Mature, processed mRNA ready for translation
The primary transcript (pre-mRNA) produced in the nucleus before processing
Small RNA involved in chromatin remodeling

Explanation.content:
hnRNA — Pre-mRNA

The answer is B. hnRNA (heterogeneous nuclear RNA) is the primary RNA transcript, containing both introns and exons. It must be processed (5' cap added, 3' poly-A tail added, introns spliced out) before becoming mature mRNA.

mRNA maturation steps:
1. Transcription → hnRNA (pre-mRNA)
2. 5' capping (7-methylguanosine cap)
3. 3' polyadenylation (poly-A tail, ~200 A residues)
4. Splicing: introns removed by spliceosome
5. Mature mRNA exported to cytoplasm for translation

Answer.content: The primary transcript (pre-mRNA) produced in the nucleus before processing

Question.content: Small nuclear RNA (snRNA) is specifically involved in:
options: Carrying amino acids to the ribosome

Explanation.content:
snRNA & The Spliceosome

The answer is Splicing of introns from pre-mRNA. snRNAs combine with proteins to form snRNPs (small nuclear ribonucleoproteins), which assemble into the spliceosome — the molecular machine that removes introns and joins exons.

Key snRNA types in the spliceosome:
U1, U2, U4, U5, U6 snRNA — each recognizes specific splice site sequences in pre-mRNA to coordinate precise intron removal.

Question.content: The 5' cap added to eukaryotic mRNA during processing consists of:

Explanation.content:
The 5' Cap

The answer is A 7-methylguanosine residue. This modified guanosine is added in reverse (5'→5') linkage at the beginning of the mRNA.

Functions of the 5' cap:
1. Protects mRNA from degradation by 5' exonucleases
2. Needed for ribosome recognition and initiation of translation
3. Promotes nuclear export of mature mRNA

Don't confuse the 5' cap with the 3' poly-A tail (which is made of adenosine residues and also protects mRNA).

Question.content: All amino acids share a common structure consisting of:
options: A central carbon with an amino group, carboxyl group, hydrogen, and R group
A central nitrogen with two carboxyl groups
A benzene ring with an amino group
A phosphate group and amino group

Explanation.content:
General Amino Acid Structure

The answer is A. Every amino acid has a central alpha-carbon (Cα) bonded to four groups. The R group (side chain) is what differs between the 20 standard amino acids.

        NH₂
         |
R — Cα — COOH
         |
         H
At physiological pH (7.4): the –NH₂ is protonated (–NH₃⁺) and –COOH is deprotonated (–COO⁻) → zwitterion form

Answer.content: A central carbon with an amino group, carboxyl group, hydrogen, and R group

Question.content: Amino acids with nonpolar, hydrophobic R groups are typically found:
options: On the outer surface of water-soluble proteins
Buried in the interior of globular proteins, away from water
In the active site of all enzymes
Only in membrane-spanning helices of integral proteins

Explanation.content:
Hydrophobic Effect in Protein Folding

The answer is B. Nonpolar amino acids (e.g., valine, leucine, isoleucine) avoid water and cluster in the protein core — this hydrophobic effect is one of the main driving forces of protein folding.

Amino acid classification by R group:
• Nonpolar/hydrophobic — buried in protein core
• Polar uncharged — surface and active sites
• Positively charged — Arg, Lys, His (basic)
• Negatively charged — Asp, Glu (acidic)

Answer.content: Buried in the interior of globular proteins, away from water

Question.content: The primary structure of a protein refers to:
options: The folding of the polypeptide into alpha helices and beta sheets
The overall 3D shape of a single polypeptide
The linear sequence of amino acids joined by peptide bonds
The arrangement of multiple polypeptide subunits

Explanation.content:
Four Levels of Protein Structure

The answer is C. Primary structure is the most basic level — simply the order of amino acids from N-terminus to C-terminus, determined by the gene sequence.

All four levels:
1. Primary — amino acid sequence (peptide bonds) ✅
2. Secondary — local folding: α-helix, β-sheet (hydrogen bonds)
3. Tertiary — overall 3D shape of single polypeptide
4. Quaternary — multiple polypeptide subunits together

Answer.content: The linear sequence of amino acids joined by peptide bonds

Question.content: The alpha-helix and beta-pleated sheet are examples of which level of protein structure?

Explanation.content:
Secondary Structure

The answer is Secondary structure. Both alpha-helices and beta-sheets arise from hydrogen bonding between atoms in the polypeptide backbone (between –NH and C=O groups of different residues).

Comparison:
• α-helix — coiled spring shape; hydrogen bonds between residues 4 apart; right-handed
• β-sheet — flat, pleated arrangement; H-bonds between adjacent chains (parallel or antiparallel)

Both are stabilized ONLY by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms — NOT R groups.

Question.content: Disulfide bonds (S–S) that stabilize tertiary protein structure form between two residues of:

Explanation.content:
Disulfide Bonds

The answer is Cysteine. Two cysteine residues can be oxidized to form a covalent disulfide bond (–S–S–) between their thiol (–SH) groups. This is one of the strongest bonds stabilizing protein 3D structure.

Forces stabilizing tertiary structure:
1. Hydrophobic interactions (strongest contributor)
2. Hydrogen bonds
3. Ionic (electrostatic) interactions
4. Disulfide bonds (covalent — strongest per bond)
5. Van der Waals forces

Question.content: Hemoglobin is a classic example of a protein with quaternary structure. It consists of:
options: Two alpha and two beta subunits (α₂β₂)

Explanation.content:
Hemoglobin — Quaternary Structure

The answer is Two alpha and two beta subunits (α₂β₂). Quaternary structure requires at least two polypeptide chains. Hemoglobin has four subunits — a tetramer — each containing a heme group with iron (Fe²⁺) that binds oxygen.

Compare:
• Myoglobin — single chain (no quaternary structure) — stores O₂ in muscle
• Hemoglobin — tetramer α₂β₂ (has quaternary structure) — transports O₂ in blood

Cooperative binding in hemoglobin (sigmoidal curve) vs myoglobin (hyperbolic) arises directly from its quaternary structure.

Answer.content: Two alpha and two beta subunits (α₂β₂)

Question.content: Denaturation of a protein involves:
options: Disruption of secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure without breaking peptide bonds

Explanation.content:
Protein Denaturation

The answer is B. Denaturation unfolds a protein by disrupting non-covalent interactions (hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, ionic bonds), but the primary structure (peptide bonds) remains intact.

Causes of denaturation:
• Heat (disrupts hydrogen bonds)
• Extreme pH (disrupts ionic interactions)
• Urea or guanidinium chloride (unfold the chain)
• Detergents (disrupt hydrophobic core)

Denaturation is often irreversible. Renaturation (refolding) can occur if the denaturing agent is gently removed.

Answer.content: Disruption of secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure without breaking peptide bonds

Question.content: According to the induced fit model of enzyme action, when a substrate enters the active site:
options: The enzyme remains rigid and unchanged
The enzyme undergoes a conformational change to better fit the substrate
The substrate is rejected if it does not match exactly

Explanation.content:
Induced Fit Model (Koshland, 1958)

The answer is C. In the induced fit model, the enzyme is flexible and its active site reshapes itself around the substrate upon binding — like a hand fitting into a glove.

Two models comparison:
• Lock-and-key (Fischer, 1894) — rigid active site; substrate fits exactly like a key into a lock
• Induced fit (Koshland, 1958) — flexible active site; enzyme changes shape upon substrate binding ✅

Modern evidence supports induced fit as the more accurate model.

Answer.content: The enzyme undergoes a conformational change to better fit the substrate

Question.content: Enzymes are classified by the International Union of Biochemistry into six classes. Which class catalyzes oxidation-reduction reactions?

Explanation.content:
Enzyme Classification (EC Numbers)

The answer is Oxidoreductases. They catalyze electron transfer reactions (oxidation-reduction).

The 6 enzyme classes:
1. Oxidoreductases — redox reactions (e.g., dehydrogenases, oxidases) ✅
2. Transferases — transfer functional groups (e.g., kinases, transaminases)
3. Hydrolases — hydrolysis reactions (e.g., lipases, proteases)
4. Lyases — break bonds without water or redox (e.g., decarboxylases)
5. Isomerases — rearrange atoms within a molecule
6. Ligases — join molecules using ATP (e.g., DNA ligase)

Question.content: On a Michaelis-Menten curve (reaction rate vs substrate concentration), Vmax is the point where:
options: All enzyme active sites are saturated with substrate
The substrate concentration is at its lowest

Explanation.content:
Vmax — Maximum Velocity

The answer is B. Vmax is the theoretical maximum reaction rate, reached when ALL enzyme molecules have their active sites occupied by substrate (enzyme is fully saturated). Adding more substrate beyond this point does not increase the rate.

Michaelis-Menten equation:
\( V = \frac{V_{max} [S]}{K_m + [S]} \)

• At [S] very low → V ≈ (Vmax/Km)[S] (linear)
• At [S] = Km → V = Vmax/2
• At [S] >> Km → V ≈ Vmax (plateau)

Answer.content: All enzyme active sites are saturated with substrate

Question.content: Non-competitive inhibition differs from competitive inhibition in that the inhibitor:
options: Binds to the active site and directly blocks substrate binding
Increases the Km without affecting Vmax
Binds to an allosteric site and reduces Vmax without changing Km
Can be overcome by increasing substrate concentration

Explanation.content:
Non-Competitive Inhibition

The answer is C. A non-competitive inhibitor binds at an allosteric site (not the active site). This changes the enzyme's shape, reducing its catalytic efficiency (lower Vmax). Since it doesn't compete for the active site, substrate can still bind — so Km is unchanged.

FeatureCompetitiveNon-competitive
BindsActive siteAllosteric site
KmIncreasesNo change
VmaxNo changeDecreases
Overcome by ↑[S]?YesNo

Answer.content: Binds to an allosteric site and reduces Vmax without changing Km

Question.content: An example of competitive enzyme inhibition with clinical relevance is the use of:
options: Statins to inhibit HMG-CoA reductase in cholesterol synthesis
Penicillin to activate bacterial cell wall synthesis

Explanation.content:
Clinical Example of Competitive Inhibition

The answer is B. Statins (e.g., lovastatin, simvastatin) mimic the natural substrate of HMG-CoA reductase — the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis — and competitively inhibit it, lowering blood cholesterol.

Other clinical competitive inhibitors:
• Methotrexate — inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (cancer treatment)
• Sulfonamides — inhibit bacterial PABA-using enzyme (antibiotics)
• ACE inhibitors — inhibit angiotensin-converting enzyme (blood pressure)

Answer.content: Statins to inhibit HMG-CoA reductase in cholesterol synthesis

Question.content: A coenzyme is best described as:
options: A protein that regulates enzyme activity
A non-protein organic molecule that is required for enzyme activity
An inorganic metal ion that activates enzymes
A lipid that assists membrane-bound enzymes

Explanation.content:
Coenzymes vs Cofactors

The answer is B. Coenzymes are non-protein organic molecules that bind temporarily (or permanently as prosthetic groups) to enzymes to help them function.

Terminology breakdown:
• Cofactor — general term: anything non-protein needed by enzyme
• Coenzyme — organic cofactor (e.g., NAD⁺, FAD, CoA, Pyridoxal phosphate)
• Metal ion cofactor — inorganic ions (e.g., Zn²⁺ in carbonic anhydrase, Fe²⁺ in hemoglobin)
• Prosthetic group — tightly bound cofactor (permanently attached)

Answer.content: A non-protein organic molecule that is required for enzyme activity

Question.content: Irreversible enzyme inhibition, as seen with nerve agents (organophosphates) and aspirin, involves:
options: Non-covalent binding to the active site
Covalent modification of a key residue in the active site
Competing with the substrate for the active site

Explanation.content:
Irreversible Inhibition

The answer is B. Irreversible inhibitors form stable covalent bonds with amino acid residues in the active site, permanently inactivating the enzyme.

⚠️ Important examples:
• Organophosphates (nerve agents, e.g., sarin) — covalently modify serine in acetylcholinesterase → acetylcholine accumulates → continuous nerve stimulation → death
• Aspirin — acetylates serine in cyclooxygenase (COX) → permanently inhibits prostaglandin synthesis → anti-inflammatory, anti-clotting effect

Answer.content: Covalent modification of a key residue in the active site

Question.content: Allosteric regulation of an enzyme occurs when a regulatory molecule binds:
options: At the active site, directly blocking substrate
At a site other than the active site, changing enzyme conformation and activity
Outside the cell, signaling DNA to stop transcription
To the substrate, preventing it from entering the active site

Explanation.content:
Allosteric Regulation

The answer is B. Allosteric regulators (activators or inhibitors) bind at a non-catalytic allosteric site, triggering a conformational change that either enhances or reduces catalytic activity.

Allosteric regulation importance:
• Fine-tunes metabolic pathways in real time
• Often involves feedback inhibition — the end product of a pathway inhibits the first enzyme in that pathway (prevents overproduction)
• Example: ATP inhibits phosphofructokinase-1 in glycolysis when energy is high

Answer.content: At a site other than the active site, changing enzyme conformation and activity
